metadata.title: Why More Automation Creates More Supervision Work, Not Less
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of claude-agentic-ai candidate 02. Bainbridge's Irony only — automation shifts human work upstream and into checkpoints, it does not eliminate human work.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = agent capability / automated work; CRIMSON #BF3339 = supervisory load / human decision work; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO history of industrial automation; NO Parasuraman levels-of-automation formalism; NO discussion of specific Claude surfaces. Mechanism: Bainbridge's Irony — more capable agents create more demanding supervisory roles, not less work.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A developer delegates bug repair to her AI agent. The agent reads files, proposes a fix, edits the code, reruns the tests. It reports: resolved.
beats.scene_description: a code editor screen on newsprint ground — green test results, a resolved status; editorial collage treatment, desaturated

beats.narration_text: Now she must read a diff, verify the test results, decide whether an edge case was covered, and check whether any files outside the reported scope were touched. That is more deliberate evaluation work than the original copy-paste workflow required.

production_viz.label: supervisor tasks stack up after the agent finishes
production_viz.mechanic: four task chips appear one by one in CRIMSON below the word 'supervisor': READ DIFF, VERIFY TESTS, CHECK EDGE CASES, AUDIT SCOPE — each chip slides in and stacks; a number tally increments from 1 to 4
production_viz.note: the accumulate move — human tasks are not fewer, they are different

beats.narration_text: More capable agents should reduce human effort on a task. Here is the case where agent capability increased the demands on the human supervisor. Why does the more capable agent create more human work?

card.copy: Why does a more capable agent create more supervisory work, not less?

beats.narration_text: The naive picture: automation replaces work. One lever goes up — agent capability — and the workload goes down. The human does less.

production_viz.label: naive lever-scale: capability up, workload down
production_viz.mechanic: a simple lever-scale: left arm labeled 'agent capability' rises (TEAL weight grows); right arm labeled 'human workload' falls — the naive model
production_viz.note: establish the naive expectation before breaking it

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] naive lever-scale: capability up, workload down

beats.narration_text: But automation does not eliminate work — it relocates it. The human's role shifts upstream into design, and at checkpoints into verification. Lisanne Bainbridge named this in 1983: the more capable the automated system, the more demanding the supervisory role.

card.copy: Automation relocates work. It does not remove it.

beats.narration_text: The real scale: as agent capability rises, supervisory load rises equally. Not because the agent is worse — but because its output has more reach. A wider action surface means more to verify, more to scope, and harder decisions when something unexpected occurs.

production_viz.label: real lever-scale: both arms rise together
production_viz.mechanic: the same lever-scale but both arms rise together — TEAL left (agent capability) and CRIMSON right (supervisory load) — neither side goes down; the scale stays level but both weights grow
production_viz.note: the Bainbridge Irony visualized: both sides grow simultaneously

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] real scale: capability and supervisory load rise together

beats.narration_text: Without an agent, Priya moves twenty files manually — each decision takes a second. With an agent reorganizing two hundred files, she must define a scope statement, review a taxonomy, approve batches, verify counts, and audit spot-checks. Calendar time is shorter. Deliberate-decision count is higher.

production_viz.label: Priya: manual vs agent — fewer hours, more decisions
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'WITHOUT AGENT' shows 20 TEAL file squares + label '20 moves, 20 decisions'; right 'WITH AGENT' shows a TEAL robot icon + a column of 5 CRIMSON decision chips (SCOPE, TAXONOMY, BATCHES, COUNTS, AUDIT) — more deliberate decisions
production_viz.note: illustrative example — numbers labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Priya: without vs with agent decision count

beats.narration_text: The supervisory role is harder because the stakes are higher. The agent reorganized two hundred files from one decision — a wrong scope statement at the start is two hundred wrong moves. Manual work fails locally. Agent work fails at scale.

card.copy: Agent work fails at scale. Manual work fails locally.

beats.narration_text: The practice: write the scope before you delegate. Before the agent starts, define what it can touch, what it must not touch, and what you will check when it finishes. The scope statement is not friction — it is the work the agent's capability moved upstream.

production_viz.label: scope moves upstream before delegation
production_viz.mechanic: a timeline arrow pointing right; three zones labeled 'BEFORE' (TEAL: scope / can touch / must not touch), 'DURING' (agent runs, small TEAL box), 'AFTER' (CRIMSON: what to check); the BEFORE zone is wider than expected — the work lived there now
production_viz.note: the concrete practice: upstream scope work is the new human role

beats.narration_text: More capable agents should reduce human effort on a task. They do not — they relocate it. The work moves upstream into design and into checkpoints. That is not less work. It is harder work in different places.

card.copy: Automation relocates human work. It does not remove it.
